clusters.title: Family of 14yo says Aidan Becker 'risked his life to save our son'
clusters.neutral_headline: Family of 14yo says Aidan Becker 'risked his life to save our son'
bullet_summary: "He showed extraordinary courage and compassion in risking his life to save our son

items.title: Family of 14yo says Aidan Becker 'risked his life to save our son'
items.standfirst: The family of a 14-year-old boy allegedly attacked in Mernda last week said Aidan Becker, who was fatally stabbed trying to protect the teenager, showed "extraordinary courage and compassion in risking his life to save our son".
items.content: The family of a 14-year-old boy allegedly attacked in Mernda last week said Aidan Becker, who was fatally stabbed trying to protect the teenager, showed "extraordinary courage and compassion in risking his life to save our son".

items.title: Family of boy who security guard died trying to protect share 'heartbreak'
items.standfirst: "He showed extraordinary courage and compassion in risking his life to save our son."
items.content: The family of a boy who was protected from an attempted robbery by a security guard who was then allegedly murdered have said they wish to honour his "bravery and kindness".Aidan Becker, 22, died after he came to the aid of a 14-year-old schoolboy at Mernda Station in Melbourne's north-east on Friday night, with four teenagers allegedly stabbing him with machetes.Becker died at the scene, and the family of the boy have expressed their sorrow at his tragic death after he protected their son.JUST IN: 'Expected': Fuel shortage to drive up grocery prices as inflation bites "We are deeply saddened by this heartbreaking loss," they said in a statement shared to 9news."He showed extraordinary courage and compassion in risking his life to save our son.""We hold your family in our thoughts and prayers."All four teenagers alleged to be involved in Becker's stabbing have now been charged with murder and armed robbery. A family friend of the Beckers has called for action, not words, from the Victorian government.Emad, a co-worker and friend of Aidan Becker's mother, Natalie, recalled the moment she called him to reveal what happened to her son.Emad said he was watching the football when he received a call he admitted "traumatised" him."By the time I went to call her, I received a message [saying], 'Call me, they killed my son,' he revealed to 3AW's Jacqui Felgate."When I rang her and then I saw her, you just visualise, 'what would you do in this situation?'WORLD: 'Weapon of mass destruction' hurled near mayor"This wasn't a gang versus gang attack. This is a beautiful boy who went to the aid to try to save a 14-year-old boy."LIVE UPDATES: Trump calls war with Iran a 'little excursion' The Victorian Government is attempting to crack down on crime, including by giving harsher sentences to children who commit violent crimes in an initiative called "Adult Crime, Adult Time".Emad urged the government to act."There's an opportunity now to lead by example and not just use it as a slogan," he said."What people want is for tragedies like this to lead to conversations… I'm not sure what the policy will become, but I just think whatever we're doing at the moment isn't working."https://omny.fm/shows/3aw-drive/family-friend-of-good-samaritan-hacked-to-death-delivers-powerful-message/embed?media=Audio&size=WideNEVER MISS A STORY: Get your breaking news and exclusive stories first by following us across all platforms.Download the 9NEWS App here via Apple and Google PlayMake 9News your preferred source on Google by ticking this box hereSign up to our breaking newsletter here

clusters.title: Residents urged to evacuate immediately as hundreds of homes expected to be flooded
clusters.neutral_headline: Residents urged to evacuate immediately as hundreds of homes expected to be flooded

items.title: Residents urged to evacuate immediately as hundreds of homes expected to be flooded
items.standfirst: The city of B undaberg on Queensland's central coast is bracing for major flooding similar to the 2010 disaster, with an emergency alert this afternoon urging residents to "leave immediately".
items.content: The city of B undaberg on Queensland's central coast is bracing for major flooding similar to the 2010 disaster, with an emergency alert this afternoon urging residents to "leave immediately".Bundaberg mayor Ellen Blackburn warned this afternoon that 280 homes and 120 businesses are expected to be inundated by tomorrow morning."If the 2010 floods affected you then you need to consider leaving your property immediately and finding safe haven," Blackburn said.READ MORE: 'Hard not to panic': Australian farmers hit first as fuel supplies dry upPhotos posted to social media show Targo Street in Bundaberg Central was already underwater at 8am AEST.Streets at risk are being door-knocked by council staff and emergency services. Four state schools and five independent schools in the area are closed. This afternoon an emergency alert was upgraded to' leave immediately' for anyone in Bundaberg North, East, Central and West. The local disaster management group has warned that bridges connecting north and south Bundaberg will close when the Burnett River reaches the major flood level of 7 metres, which is anticipated by 3pm AEST.The river is expected to rise further to 7.6 metres, just below the 2010 flood level of 7.92 metres, according to the Bureau of Meteorology.Once the bridges close, there will no safe evacuation routes and residents will be isolated until floodwaters recede, the disaster management group warned. Power supply could be lost and those with medical needs in Bundaberg North have been warned they will have no access to healthcare if they do not evacuate now. About 10,000 people live in Bundaberg North. LIVE UPDATES: Australia to send high-tech military plane and missiles to Middle EastPremier David Crisafulli said the gauge upstream at Walla had reached 19 metres this afternoon, a key indicator of the impact to come for Bundaberg. " There will be a large number of homes that will be impacted overnight into the early hours of tomorrow ," Crisafulli said." If you get a message from authorities, if somebody knocks on your door, if you can seek shelter with friends and family at higher ground if you are one of those properties that is below 7.5 metres, that is good advice that should be heeded."Yes ,there will be challenges but this is a really mighty community. " To see the impacts of (the 2013 flood) and the way that people mobilised shows me everything I need to know about this community." This event won't be as big as 2013 but I bet you the response and the compassion and the ticker of this community will be just as big and they've proven it time and time again." There are people who will be impacted in the next 24 hours that it might be the fourth time in 15 years and yet they respond in the true Queensland way."READ MORE: Five Iranian footballers granted asylum by Australia after escapeDuring the 2010 flood disaster, hundreds of people were evacuated and homes and business es in Bundaberg North and East were inundated.The recreational precinct on Kendalls Rd in Branyan has been designated as a place of refuge but authorities are urging people to stay with family and friends if possible.It is only a place of refuge, not an evacuation centre, so resources will be limited. The catchment has been smashed with heavy rain since Friday morning , brought on by a tropical low moving over the state which has now mostly contracted offshore.The Wide Bay and Burnett region has copped the brunt of the heavy rain, however flooding is either occurring or possible in much of Queensland. An enhanced image created by Weatherzone shows the widespread slow-moving rain storms that has left much of the state wet or saturated. Some of the highest rainfall totals yesterday were at Westwood Range, which received 244mm in six hours yesterday afternoon, Captain Creek, which received 217mm in the same time, and Eden, where there was 146mm in just three hours and 74mm in one hour, according to Weatherzone.Authorities beg drivers to stay out of floodwater A number of swift water rescues have occurred across the region, with a man and a boy rescued from a car on flooded causeway at Bargara yesterday morning.The boy was pulled through the window of the sedan by a police officer before the driver, a 54-year-old man was also helped from the vehicle, and fined for driving without due care and attention.An off-duty police officer was also rescued from the roof of her car at Peachester on the Sunshine Coast by swift water rescue teams.READ MORE: 'God bless Australia!' Trump's praise after late-night call with Albanese In Gympie, a man spent four hours on the roof of his car, waiting for rescue, just 50 metres from where a 14-year-old girl lost her life in raging water in the 2022 flood."We've had a fair number of rescues," Matthew Bruce from Queensland Fire and Rescue said."We really urge all people on the roads just to take extra care."It's much safer to turn around, don't drown."NEVER MISS A STORY: Get your breaking news and exclusive stories first by following us across all platforms.Download the 9NEWS App here via Apple and Google PlayMake 9News your preferred source on Google by ticking this box hereSign up to our breaking newsletter here

clusters.title: $1.6 billion-a-year tax change confirmed for millions of super accounts
clusters.neutral_headline: $1.6 billion-a-year tax change confirmed for millions of super accounts

items.title: $1.6 billion-a-year tax change confirmed for millions of super accounts
items.standfirst: The federal government's proposed changes to the way superannuation is taxed will soon become law.
items.content: The federal government's proposed changes to the way superannuation is taxed will soon become law, after the Greens agreed to back the reform.Under the plan, roughly 1.3 million of the nation's lowest-paid workers will get access to the low-income superannuation tax offset (LISTO), while about 90,000 ultra-wealthy Australians will have their superannuation earnings taxed at a higher rate.The move will also raise an extra $1.6 billion a year in revenue for the federal budget.IRANFLATION: Fuel shortage to drive up grocery prices as inflation bites While the government easily has the numbers to pass legislation through the lower house, with the Coalition opposing the change, it required the support of the Greens to get the bill through the Senate.This morning, the minor party announced it will back the laws, while at the same time urging the government to pull the trigger on major tax reform in the upcoming federal budget."We're disappointed that Labor has watered down its original proposal, but we strongly support the increase to the low-income super tax offset, two-thirds of which will benefit women's retirement savings," Greens senator Nick McKim said."We are going to support the bill as a downpayment on genuine, progressive tax reform in this budget."LIVE UPDATES: Australia to send high-tech military plane and missiles to Middle EastSuperannuation changes to start July 1With the laws set to pass the Senate, the superannuation changes will come into effect at the start of the next financial year.From July 1, the LISTO threshold will be raised to offset the tax paid on superannuation for anyone earning less than $45,000 a year, up from $37,000, effectively ensuring super is still tax-effective for Australians on low incomes.Raising the threshold to the top of the second income tax bracket will cover an additional 1.3 million workers.According to the Association of Superannuation Funds of Australia (ASFA), that change will deliver someone on $44,000 a year close to an extra $50,000 in their super balance by the time they retire.READ MORE: Five Iranian footballers granted asylum by Australia after escapeThe other change impacts ultra-wealthy Australians by increasing the tax rate on superannuation earnings for accounts worth more than $3 million.This will impact the top 0.3 per cent of super account holders – a total of about 90,000 people.Instead of the current flat concessional tax rate of 15 per cent, from July 1, the tax rate on earnings for super accounts with a balance of between $3 million and $10 million will rise to 30 per cent, and to 40 per cent for accounts worth more than $10 million.The increased tax rates won't apply to an account's entire earnings – only to the proportion of the balance over each of the new thresholds.NATIONAL: 'Hard not to panic': Australian farmers hit first as fuel supplies dry upGreens push for wider tax changes in budgetBy describing their backing as a down payment on progressive reform, the Greens have only raised speculation further that the May 12 federal government will contain some of the most substantial changes to the tax system in decades."This budget is a once-in-a-generation opportunity for ambitious tax reform, and we are opening the door for Labor to walk through," McKim said.Exactly what will be unveiled remains a mystery, but the government has done little, if anything, to quash rumours of the controversial capital gains tax discount being pared back for investment properties – something McKim alluded to."The current tax system has turbo-charged the housing crisis, wealth inequality and a deepening intergenerational divide," he said. "We are demanding bold reform that responds to these challenges and makes our society fairer for young people and working people.EXCLUSIVE: Andy paid his parking fine, only to be slapped with another $130 fine"There is a massive Labor majority in the House of Representatives, the opposition is a rabble, and the numbers are there in the Senate as long as Labor shows courage. "The only limit is Labor's level of ambition."Treasurer Jim Chalmers has frequently spoken of the government's desire to address intergenerational inequality.Last night, The Australian reported Treasury has conducted modelling on limiting negative gearing to two investment properties, while there have also been rumours of tax incentives for electric vehicles and home batteries being scaled back to cut down on federal spending levels.Last Friday, Chalmers hosted a roundtable discussion with a group of leading private economists, which he said would help inform the decisions made in the budget.NEVER MISS A STORY: Get your breaking news and exclusive stories first by following us across all platforms.Download the 9NEWS App here via Apple and Google PlayMake 9News your preferred source on Google by ticking this box hereSign up to our breaking newsletter here

items.standfirst: As missiles and drones began lighting up the sky over Dubai, many of the city's social media personalities started posting about it. Then the government stepped in.
items.content: As missiles and drones began lighting up the sky over Dubai, many of the city's social media personalities started posting about it. Then the government stepped in.

clusters.title: Forwarded email a rare public glimpse into the cunning world of political spin
clusters.neutral_headline: Forwarded email a rare public glimpse into the cunning world of political spin

items.title: Forwarded email a rare public glimpse into the cunning world of political spin
items.standfirst: It is common for media advisors to provide reporters with background information in an effort to shape the way a story is reported, but the practice backfired spectacularly when the SA government shared an email from the wrong patient.
items.content: It is common for media advisors to provide reporters with background information in an effort to shape the way a story is reported, but the practice backfired spectacularly when the SA government shared an email from the wrong patient.

clusters.title: Officials intercept 150,000 melatonin units at border, amid broader crackdown
clusters.neutral_headline: Officials intercept 150,000 melatonin units at border, amid broader crackdown

items.title: Officials intercept 150,000 melatonin units at border, amid broader crackdown
items.standfirst: Australian Border Force officials have intercepted more than 150,000 units of melatonin in four months, after authorities warned they would crack down on unregulated products.
items.content: Australian Border Force officials have intercepted more than 150,000 units of melatonin in four months, after authorities warned they would crack down on unregulated products.

items.title: ABC Shakespeare quiz: How well doest thou know his characters and quotes?
items.standfirst: A quiz by any other word would smell as sweet, or something, so have a crack at our 10 questions on the works of William Shakespeare.

clusters.title: Will this year's flu vaccine protect against the fast-moving Super-K strain?
clusters.neutral_headline: Will this year's flu vaccine protect against the fast-moving Super-K strain

items.title: Will this year's flu vaccine protect against the fast-moving Super-K strain?
items.standfirst: Experts say they are feeling "nervous" as Australia approached this year's flu season, with a new, fast-moving strain of influenza dubbed "Super-K" circulating as vaccine uptake dwindles.
items.content: Experts say they are feeling "nervous" as Australia approached this year's flu season, with a new, fast-moving strain of influenza dubbed "Super-K" circulating as vaccine uptake dwindles.
